articles.title: Cloud Bills Took a Decade to Get FinOps. AI Bills Won't Get That Long.
articles.excerpt: Watch what happened this month. Databricks moved Genie to pay-as-you-go. Every user gets a small free allowance, then the meter runs. And notice what shipped the same day: budgets, alerts, per-user sp…
articles.body: Watch what happened this month. Databricks moved Genie to pay-as-you-go. Every user gets a small free allowance, then the meter runs. And notice what shipped the same day : budgets, alerts, per-user spending limits. Not a follow-up release. Not "coming in Q4." The governance shipped with the meter. That detail is the whole story, and it took the industry ten years of cloud pain to learn it. Let me show you the pattern — and then let's compute the numbers your CFO is going to ask you for, before they ask. The three acts (every computing wave repeats them) I teach my MBA students that every computing wave walks through the same three acts. Act one: adoption without meters. Everyone experiments, nobody measures. Engineering spins up assistants, agents, RAG pipelines.
articles.author: Vinicius Fagundes

articles.title: 🦊 Build Your Own Codex Buddy: Create a Custom Animated Coding Pet
articles.excerpt: Imagine having a tiny digital companion sitting beside your code editor, reacting whenever Codex thinks, processes code, or completes a task. With Codex's custom pet feature, you can create your own u…
articles.body: Imagine having a tiny digital companion sitting beside your code editor, reacting whenever Codex thinks, processes code, or completes a task. With Codex's custom pet feature, you can create your own unique coding buddy in just a few steps. Step 1: Download the Codex Desktop App Visit codex.openai.com and download the Codex Desktop App. The custom pet feature is available in the desktop application, not the web version. Step 2: Install the Pet Skill Open the Codex chat and run the following command: $skill -installer hatch-pet This installs the skill required to generate custom animated pets. Step 3: Reload the Skills Press: Ctrl + K (Windows) Cmd + K (Mac) Then click Force Reload Skills to activate the newly installed skill. Step 4: Describe Your Pet Now it's time to get creative.
articles.author: uttesh

articles.title: How I built a high-performance Chinese learning platform with 100k+ TV clips using Rust and Next.js 15
articles.excerpt: Hi dev.to community! 👋 Today, I’m incredibly excited to share MandarinClips, a side project I’ve been pouring my soul into. It's a high-performance language learning platform that indexes and organiz…
articles.body: Hi dev.to community! 👋 Today, I’m incredibly excited to share MandarinClips , a side project I’ve been pouring my soul into. It's a high-performance language learning platform that indexes and organizes sentence-sized dialogue segments from authentic Chinese movies and TV series to help language learners transition from sterile textbooks to real-world immersion. 👉 Check it out here: MandarinClips.com Here is a deep dive into the engineering decisions, performance bottlenecks, and the technical architecture that powers the platform under the hood. 🏗️ The High-Level Architecture I wanted the platform to be blindingly fast, cost-effective, and highly available. Here is the stack: Frontend : Next.js 15 (React 19) + Tailwind CSS + next-intl (for multilingual support).
articles.author: Vincent

articles.title: Human-in-the-Loop for AI Products: When the Model Decides and When a Person Does
articles.excerpt: Fully autonomous AI systems in production make critical mistakes. Adding human-in-the-loop reduces them by an order of magnitude, but increases latency and processing cost. The question is not whether…
articles.body: Fully autonomous AI systems in production make critical mistakes. Adding human-in-the-loop reduces them by an order of magnitude, but increases latency and processing cost. The question is not whether a human is needed in the loop. The question is exactly where to place them so they catch dangerous decisions without turning the AI system into an expensive interface for manual work. When autonomy works and when it kills the product Two extremes. Full autonomy: the model decides everything, humans see the result after the fact. Full control: every model response is manually reviewed before reaching the user. The first scales, but destroys trust when errors occur. The second is reliable, but kills the economics. Real AI products live between these poles.
articles.author: Roman Belov

articles.title: AWS Lambda Self-Managed Code Storage: Adiós al límite de 75GB 🪣
articles.excerpt: Contexto En proyectos donde se despliegan muchas funciones Lambda y capas (layers) con dependencias pesadas, el límite de almacenamiento de código por región empieza a doler. El límite histórico era d…
articles.body: Contexto En proyectos donde se despliegan muchas funciones Lambda y capas ( layers ) con dependencias pesadas, el límite de almacenamiento de código por región empieza a doler. El límite histórico era de 75GB por región por cuenta que parece bastante pero sabemos que cada vez se necesitan grandes cantidades de datos en formato de código fuente o librerías, y ampliarlo implicaba abrir un ticket de soporte para solicitar un aumento de cuota. No es el proceso más ágil del mundo, (tampoco el más complicado!!!). Sin embargo, el 17 de julio de 2026, AWS publicó en el Compute Blog los detalles de self-managed code storage para AWS Lambda . En este post exploro qué es, cómo funciona y cuándo tiene sentido usarla. ¿Qué es Lambda Self-Managed Code Storage?
articles.author: olcortesb

articles.title: C++ struct vs class — The Only Difference That Matters (Plus Real Use Cases)
articles.excerpt: Hi everyone! I'm AlanWu, a junior high school C++ learner. When I first saw both struct and class in C++, I was confused. I thought struct was just a C thing — a bag of variables with no functions. Bu…
articles.body: Hi everyone! I'm AlanWu, a junior high school C++ learner. When I first saw both struct and class in C++, I was confused. I thought struct was just a C thing — a bag of variables with no functions. But in C++, you can put methods, constructors, inheritance — everything — in a struct. So what's the actual difference? Here's the one-sentence answer, plus practical guidance on when to use which. My GitHub: https://github.com/Cn-Alanwu The One-Sentence Difference In C++, struct and class are identical in every way — except the default access level: struct members are public by default; class members are private by default. That's it. Everything else — inheritance, constructors, destructors, member functions, templates, virtual functions — works exactly the same in both.
articles.author: Yilong Wu

articles.title: NoWreck by AstralXVoid
articles.excerpt: NoWreck / NoWreck NoWreck A deterministic verifier for AI coding assistants. When an AI coding tool changes your code, it also tells you what it did. Sometimes that explanation is wrong — it reference…
articles.body: NoWreck AstralXVoid / NoWreck A CLI tool that verifies AI coding assistant claims against actual structural changes — catching hallucinated functions, fake calls, and missed modifications before they ship. NoWreck A deterministic verifier for AI coding assistants. When an AI coding tool changes your code, it also tells you what it did. Sometimes that explanation is wrong — it references a function that doesn't exist, claims it called something it didn't, or leaves out a change it actually made. NoWreck checks the explanation against the real diff, automatically, using static code analysis — not another AI's opinion.
articles.author: AstralXVoid

articles.title: House of the Dragon season 3 episode 5 explained: Who’s killing the Gold Cloaks?
articles.excerpt: The latest threat to Rhaenyra Targaryen's reign is a string of grisly murders targeting the city watch. And the mystery gets very dark.
articles.body: Every time queen Rhaenyra Targaryen (Emma D’Arcy) has a council meeting with her advisors, they spend most of the meeting bickering about what the realm’s biggest problems are these days. Is it the crown’s lack of funding? The lingering threat of Aemond (Ewan Mitchell) and his dragon Vhagar? Ormund Hightower (James Norton)’s hostile takeover of Tumbleton? Or civil unrest amongst the smallfolk? In House of the Dragon ’s latest episode, Daemon Targaryen (Matt Smith) brings yet another big problem to the council, and it’s one that hits very close to home: Somebody is killing Gold Cloaks, members of the city guard. But who?
articles.author: Corey Plante

articles.title: House of the Dragon finally solves a political mystery that Game of Thrones never did
articles.excerpt: The latest House of the Dragon episode revealed why House Martell of Dorne might be the answer to the destruction of the Targaryens
articles.body: George R.R. Martin ’s fantasy books set in the world of Westeros are full of political intrigue, and HBO has brought that to life with relative ease in Game of Thrones , House of the Dragon , and even in the more commoner-oriented TV series, A Knight of the Seven Kingdoms . From Aegon’s Conquest of Westeros to Robert’s Rebellion against the Targaryen dynasty, the fragile alliances and endless scheming that hold Westeros together are a big part of what keeps audiences coming back for more, and obsessing over the story in between new seasons.
articles.author: Aimee Hart

articles.title: Call of Duty Ambience Website Lets You Fall Asleep to Nostalgic Black Ops and MW2 Lobbies
articles.excerpt: Have you ever wanted to fall asleep to the sound of Call of Duty players screaming through a $5 mic and into your ear? No? Well, Call of Duty Ambience is here anyway.
articles.body: Have you ever wanted to fall asleep to the sound of Call of Duty players screaming through a $5 mic and into your ear? No? Well, Call of Duty Ambience is here anyway. Erected by app-maker Mike Wing, Call of Duty Ambience is a new website that, for some reason, allows you to tap back into multiplayer lobbies like it's 2010 all over again. Fit with an Xbox 360 theme, cringy gamertags, and other classic visuals, the site allows anyone to log in and get some shuteye while strangers argue in the background. Three Call of Duty games are supported at the moment: Modern Warfare 2 , Black Ops , and Black Ops 2 . If you ever played a few games of team deathmatch for any of these first-person shooter classics, you should immediately feel right at home.
articles.author: Michael Cripe

articles.title: 10 best anime to watch if you love The Odyssey (#4 is a near-perfect masterpiece)
articles.excerpt: Some are sword-and-sandals classics, while others are homecoming adventures.
articles.body: At its core, this story — attributed to Homer — has always been about homecoming. The cunning Odysseus is forced to contend with the forces of predetermined fate, which shape his 10-year struggle to return to Ithaca and reunite with his loved ones. The meat of this tale lies in the clash between free will and destiny, along with the larger-than-life presence of gods, monsters, and everything in between.

articles.excerpt: The internet has fallen in love with a viral raccoon named Jimothy, and so has the gaming industry.
articles.body: The internet has fallen in love with a viral raccoon named Jimothy, and so has the gaming industry. In case you've somehow dodged this adorable little friend on social media, Jimothy is a small raccoon in Seattle who has scurried his way through X/Twitter, Reddit, Facebook, and just about every other online platform you can think of. His unique size, seemingly caused by what many believe is a rare condition called Short Spine Syndrome, has made him hard to miss, and almost impossible to forget. Locals seem to have taken notice of this specific masked critter before, but it wasn't until Instagram user Kiana Hall captured a video that he became the latest internet-famous animal.
articles.author: Michael Cripe

articles.title: 'Aliens' at 40: James Cameron's sequel is a sci-fi icon, but did you know he quit the movie twice?
articles.excerpt: Forty years ago, Aliens burst into our hearts, but this classic sequel nearly fell by the wayside due to some serious studio-created problems that saw James Cameron quit twice.
articles.body: Where does James Cameron's "Aliens" rank among the other Alien movies ? That depends on who you ask. Some might say it's the best, while others put it just behind the original (spoiler alert: it's the best). Regardless, there's no denying that this 1986 film shifted gears, moving the franchise from horror to horror-tinged sci-fi action and paving an alternate path for the future. As Cameron stated in an interview with Lofficier , the plan always was "to do a film that was not as scary... it's scary, but it's not as scary, but more intense, and I like to use the word, exhilarating. Because I think you get exhilarated by the intensity of the kind of action that's in this film." In terms of story progression, the sequel's shake-up makes complete sense.
articles.author: Sergio Pereira

articles.title: Should we trust AI in the search for alien life? Scientists aren't so sure
articles.excerpt: New research suggests AI can be repeatedly fooled into mistaking non-life for life.
articles.body: Artificial intelligence could easily fall into the trap of identifying non-life as life on other worlds, claim two researchers from Michigan State University who have tested AI on simulated life in a computer program. "We had previously seen that AI has a big Achilles heel when it is trying to classify things that are unlike the things in its training examples," Michigan's Christoph Adami told Space.com. "We call these 'out-of-distribution' samples and it is just incredibly easy to get AI to misclassify." Adami is a computational biologist who uses computers to apply information theory to the study of evolution and biology. One of his leading tenets is that life can be defined by its ability to encode information and replicate it.
articles.author: Keith Cooper

articles.title: How Christopher Nolan's 'Interstellar' turned general relativity into the bad guy
articles.excerpt: As Nolan embarks on "The Odyssey", we look back on the sci-fi movie where time dilation is as cruel as any Greek god.
articles.body: The biggest blockbusters of 2014 threw up a varied rogues' gallery of bad guys. In "Transformers: Age of Extinction", it was a reincarnated version of Optimus Prime's perma-nemesis, Megatron, while Captain America faced off against a reborn Hydra infiltrating the upper echelons of the US government in "The Winter Soldier". For Spider-Man, it was Electro, while Maleficent showed up in live-action to give Sleeping Beauty nightmares. Christopher Nolan has never been one to go with the Hollywood crowd, however, and in his outer space opus "Interstellar", the antagonist was time itself — or, to be more specific, Einstein's theory of general relativity. Nolan, whose Homeric epic "The Odyssey" makes landfall in theaters this week, has always been obsessed with time, and our perception of it.
articles.author: Richard Edwards

articles.title: 10 places in the US and Canada to see August's partial solar eclipse and Perseid meteor shower in 1 trip
articles.excerpt: A partial solar eclipse by day and the peak of the Perseid meteor shower by night make Aug. 12, 2026, one of the best nights for skywatching and camping in years, but only for some parts of North Amer…
articles.body: Aug. 12, 2026, offers a rare double-header for skywatchers across northeastern North America: a partial solar eclipse during the afternoon followed, hours later, by the peak of the Perseid meteor shower beneath dark summer skies. The eclipse is part of a total solar eclipse happening in eastern Greenland, western Iceland and northern Spain. "The neat thing about seeing a partial eclipse, especially one that is total somewhere else, is that you get to join in with the whole event that's going on," Tyler Nordgren, astronomer and eclipse artist at Space Art Travel Bureau , told Space.com.
articles.author: Jamie Carter

articles.title: James Webb Space Telescope discovers how black holes feed themselves
articles.excerpt: Using the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST), astronomers have gotten a glimpse at the mechanisms that supermassive black holes use to feed themselves.
articles.body: Thanks to the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST), astronomers have been given a glimpse of the mechanisms that supermassive black holes use to feed themselves. The observations could help scientists discover how supermassive black holes with masses millions, often even billions, of times that of the sun grew so fast so soon after the Big Bang. While all large galaxies have supermassive black holes at their hearts, some are slumbering giants, consuming little surrounding gas and dust, while others are ravenously feeding and acting as the engines of bright central galactic regions called active galactic nuclei (AGN).
articles.author: Robert Lea

articles.title: Scientists told them, 'No, it's too dangerous,' but they did it anyway: Inside Japan's super-close asteroid flyby
articles.excerpt: Japan's Hayabusa2 spacecraft returned spectacular images of a near-Earth asteroid following a super-close flyby on July 5, but heated debate was needed before teams signed off on the daring attempt.
articles.body: Japan's Hayabusa2 spacecraft returned spectacular images of a near-Earth asteroid following a super-close flyby on July 5, but heated debate was needed before teams signed off on the daring attempt. When images of the asteroid Torifune arrived on the morning of July 6 Japan time, Makoto Yoshikawa and his team at the Japan Aerospace Exploration Agency ( JAXA ) got two surprises at once. Torifune turned out to be a contact binary, in which two chunks of rock have come together under gravity, and the returned images were also larger than hoped. "We did not imagine such a contact binary ," Yoshikawa, former mission manager of Hayabusa2 , told scientists gathered at the Asteroids, Comets and Meteors conference in Poznan, Poland, on July 10.
articles.author: Andrew Jones

articles.title: The AI compute gap: Enterprises are buying infrastructure faster than they can measure what it costs
articles.excerpt: Across 107 enterprises, AI infrastructure spending is accelerating well ahead of the ability to see or steer its economics. Most organizations run their AI on a familiar base of hyperscalers and model…
articles.body: Across 107 enterprises, AI infrastructure spending is accelerating well ahead of the ability to see or steer its economics. Most organizations run their AI on a familiar base of hyperscalers and model-provider APIs, yet the next dollar is aimed at specialized compute almost none of them use today; a majority intend to switch or add providers within the year, many within a quarter. Buying decisions turn on integration and total cost of ownership rather than headline token price — which is fortunate, because most enterprises cannot yet see their unit economics clearly: GPUs sit at half utilization or less, and fewer than half rigorously track what their compute actually costs. The result is a compute gap — heavy, fast-moving investment running ahead of the visibility needed to control it.
articles.author: VentureBeat AI
